title: The Science Behind Gut Feelings
meta_description: Your gut feelings are real. Learn the science behind them and why ignoring them costs you.
og_description: Your gut feelings are real. Learn the science behind them and why ignoring them costs you.
body:
Your Gut is Not Being Dramatic

You know that feeling. You meet someone new and something feels off. You can't name it. You can't prove it. But your body knows. Your stomach tightens. Your shoulders pull up. There's a quiet alarm bell ringing somewhere in your chest. And then you ignore it - because that's what sensible people do, right? - and you walk right into a situation that you already knew was wrong.

I'm Kalesh. I've spent years teaching people to trust that feeling instead of explaining it away. And here's what I know for sure: nothing is wrong with you. Your nervous system isn't broken. It's calibrated for a wider range than most people's. You're picking up information that other people miss. The question isn't whether you're imagining things. The question is whether you're willing to listen.

Let's get into the science behind gut feelings. Because this isn't woo-woo. This is biology. This is survival. And once you understand how it works, you can stop second-guessing yourself and start using one of the most powerful tools you've got.

Your gut feeling isn't a guess. It's your body's way of telling you something your conscious mind hasn't caught up to yet. And it's almost never wrong - you just don't always know how to read the message.

What's Actually Happening When You Get a Gut Feeling

Let's start with the hardware. You've got a network of neurons in your digestive system - around 100 million of them. That's more than in your spinal cord. Scientists call it the enteric nervous system. I call it your second brain. It's not just processing food. It's processing information.

Here's how it works. Your gut and your brain are connected by the vagus nerve - a kind of superhighway running between your head and your belly. Information flows both ways. About 90% of that flow is from your gut up to your brain. Think about that for a second. Most of what your brain knows about the world comes through your body first, not your eyes or ears. Right?!

When you get a gut feeling, your enteric nervous system is reacting to signals your conscious mind hasn't processed yet. Maybe it's a micro-expression on someone's face. Maybe it's a change in the energy of a room. Maybe it's a pattern your brain recognized but hasn't labeled yet. Your gut picks it up and sends a signal: pay attention. Something's happening.

This isn't magic. It's pattern recognition happening below the level of conscious thought. And it's been keeping humans alive for hundreds of thousands of years.

The Body Keeps the Score - Literally

You've heard the phrase "the body keeps the score." It's true. Your body remembers everything. Every experience, every emotion, every subtle interaction - it's all stored somewhere in your nervous system. And when you encounter a situation that matches a past pattern, your body reacts before your mind catches up.

Think about it this way. If you've ever been burned by a hot stove, you don't need to think about pulling your hand away the next time you get close to heat. Your body remembers. The same thing happens with people and situations. If someone reminds you - even in a way you can't articulate - of a person who hurt you before, your gut will react. It's trying to protect you.

The problem is that most of us have been trained to override these signals. We're told to be polite. We're told not to judge. We're told to give people the benefit of the doubt. And sometimes that's the right call. But when your gut is screaming at you, it's not being dramatic. It's doing its job.

There's a book I recommend to almost everyone I work with: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk. It's the best explanation I've found for how your body stores trauma and how those stored experiences shape your gut feelings. See on Amazon (paid link)

Does that land? I hope so. Because this is the foundation of everything else we're going to talk about.

Why You've Been Taught to Ignore Your Gut

Here's where it gets personal. Most of us weren't raised in environments where our gut feelings were honored. If you're a sensitive person - and if you're reading this, you probably are - you were told some version of these things:

  • "You're too sensitive."
  • "Stop being dramatic."
  • "You're imagining things."
  • "That's not what happened."
  • "You're overreacting."

These messages come from parents, teachers, partners, friends. They come from a culture that values rational thinking over intuitive knowing. And they sink in deep. You learn to distrust yourself. You learn to override your body's signals. You learn to explain away the very thing that's trying to keep you safe.

But here's the thing. Your gut doesn't stop sending signals just because you stopped listening. It keeps trying. It keeps whispering, then talking, then shouting. And the longer you ignore it, the louder it gets. Eventually, it shows up as anxiety, as physical illness, as burnout, as that vague sense that something is wrong even when everything looks fine on paper.

I call this the Bright Wound. It's the pain of being sensitive in a world that doesn't value sensitivity. And it's real. But it's also the door to your deepest strength. Because that same sensitivity that got you wounded is also your greatest gift - if you learn to work with it instead of against it.

The Science of Interoception - How You Sense Yourself

There's a scientific term for your ability to feel what's happening inside your body. It's called interoception. It's your sense of your own internal state - your heartbeat, your breathing, your digestion, your tension. And it's the foundation of gut feelings.

Everyone has interoception, but not everyone has the same level of sensitivity. Some people can feel their heartbeat at rest. Others can't. Some people notice subtle shifts in their gut immediately. Others miss them entirely. And here's the key finding from the research: people with higher interoceptive accuracy tend to make better decisions - especially in complex, high-stakes situations.

Why? Because they're getting more information. They're not just thinking about the decision. They're feeling their body's response to it. And that response contains data that their conscious mind doesn't have access to.

Think about a time you made a decision that felt right. You probably didn't have all the facts. You couldn't explain why you chose what you chose. But you felt it in your body. That's interoception at work. That's your gut guiding you.

And the best part? You can improve your interoception. It's not a fixed trait. It's a skill you can develop. The more you practice noticing your body's signals, the more accurate and reliable they become.

How to Tell the Difference Between Gut Feeling and Anxiety

This is the question I get more than any other. "How do I know if it's a gut feeling or just my anxiety?" And it's a fair question. Because both can feel similar in the body. Both can show up as tightness, as unease, as that knot in your stomach.

Here's how I teach people to tell the difference. Gut feelings are clean. They're direct. They don't come with a story. You just know. There's no internal debate. There's no loop of "what if" thoughts. It's just a clear signal. Yes or no. Toward or away. Safe or not safe.

Anxiety, on the other hand, is noisy. It comes with a running commentary. It loops. It spirals. It says things like "but what if you're wrong" and "but you can't trust yourself" and "but you need more information." Anxiety is your mind trying to take over from your gut. And it's almost always a sign that you're not listening to your body.

A good rule of thumb: if you're asking whether it's a gut feeling or anxiety, it's probably anxiety. Real gut feelings don't need to be analyzed. They just are. They show up, you notice them, and you act. There's no middle step where you have to figure out what they mean.

That said, there's a learning curve. When you first start paying attention to your gut, it's normal to be confused. Your signals might be muddy. You might not trust what you're feeling. That's okay. It gets clearer with practice. And there are tools that can help.

One of my favorites is The Gift of Fear by Gavin de Becker. It's a brilliant book about learning to trust your intuition in dangerous situations. See on Amazon (paid link)

Practical Ways to Strengthen Your Gut Instinct

You don't need to be born with a strong gut feeling. You can build it. Here's how.

Start with small decisions

Don't try to use your gut for life-changing choices right away. Start with small things. What do you want for dinner? Which route should you take to work? Should you call that person back now or later? Notice what your body says about these small choices. The more you practice, the more you'll trust the signal.

Create space for silence

Your gut speaks in whispers. If you're constantly surrounded by noise - podcasts, social media, conversations, notifications - you won't hear it. Give yourself five minutes of silence each day. Just sit. Breathe. Notice what your body is telling you. Don't try to change anything. Just listen.

Write down your gut feelings

Keep a simple log. When you get a gut feeling, write it down. What did you feel? Where in your body? What was the situation? Then come back later and check how it turned out. This builds trust. You'll start to see patterns. You'll realize how often your gut was right and you just didn't listen.

Learn your body's language

Everyone's gut speaks differently. For some people, it's a knot in the stomach. For others, it's a tightness in the chest. Some feel a chill. Some feel heat. Pay attention to your unique signals. Learn what a "yes" feels like in your body versus a "no." They're probably different.

If you're serious about this work, I recommend Psychic Witch by Mat Auryn. It's a practical guide to developing your intuitive abilities, including gut feelings. See on Amazon (paid link)

What to Do When Your Gut Says No

This is the hard part. You get a clear signal. Your body says no. But saying no means disappointing someone. It means canceling plans. It means leaving a situation. It means having an uncomfortable conversation.

Here's what I tell everyone: a clear no from your gut is non-negotiable. You don't need to justify it. You don't need to explain it. You don't need to have a good reason. No is a complete sentence.

But that doesn't make it easy. Because you've spent years - maybe decades - overriding your no. You've been trained to be polite, to accommodate, to put other people's comfort ahead of your own safety. Unlearning that takes time.

Start small. Say no to one thing this week that your gut is telling you to skip. It doesn't have to be dramatic. Maybe it's a coffee date with someone who drains you. Maybe it's staying late at work when you know you need rest. Maybe it's agreeing to a favor you don't have the energy for. Just say no. And notice what happens. The world doesn't end. The person doesn't collapse. You survive. And you get a little stronger.

There's a book that helped me understand this deeply: The Four Agreements by Don Miguel Ruiz. It's about learning to be impeccable with your word - including the word you give to yourself. See on Amazon (paid link)

The Deeper Layer - Collective Gut Feelings

Here's something most people don't talk about. Your gut doesn't just pick up on your own patterns. It also picks up on collective energy. When you walk into a room, you can feel the tension. When you're around a group of people who are lying, you feel it in your bones. When something is off in a relationship, you know before anyone says a word.

This isn't just empathy. It's a form of energetic sensitivity. And it's more common in people who were told they were "too sensitive" as children. You weren't too sensitive. You were accurately sensitive. You were picking up on information that other people were ignoring.

The challenge is learning to distinguish between what's yours and what's not. When you feel a sudden wave of anxiety, is it yours? Or did you pick it up from the person next to you? When you feel a sinking feeling in your gut, is it about your situation? Or is it about the collective energy of the room?

This takes practice. But the basic rule is this: if the feeling came on suddenly and doesn't have a clear trigger in your life, it might not be yours. If it lingers and has a story attached to it, it's probably yours. If it comes and goes quickly, it's probably someone else's.

Trusting Yourself When Everyone Else Doubts You

This is the final piece. You can learn all the science. You can practice all the techniques. But when it comes down to it, you have to make a choice. Are you going to trust yourself? Or are you going to keep giving your power away to people who don't feel what you feel?

I'm not saying you should never listen to outside input. I'm not saying you should ignore logic or evidence. But I am saying this: your gut feeling is data. It's real information. And it deserves a seat at the table. It deserves to be considered alongside everything else.

If you're reading this and you've been doubting yourself for years, I see you. I know how exhausting it is. I know how lonely it is to feel things other people don't feel. But I also know that the same sensitivity that's been a source of pain is also your greatest strength. It's not a flaw. It's a gift. And once you learn to use it, it will guide you better than any external authority ever could.

Your gut knows. It's always known. The question is whether you're ready to listen.

If you're ready to go deeper into this work, I've created an assessment that can help you understand your intuitive style and how to trust it more fully. Take the free assessment here or try the quick quiz to see where you're at right now.
